query: reliability of family history in predicting childhood hypercholesterolemia according to current medical guidelines

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia, Type 2A(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Detection of index cases could be done by targeted screening of individuals with premature CVD, opportunistic screening in primary care based on age and gender-specific plasma LDL-C levels, or universal screening based on age and gender-specific plasma LDL-C levels before the age of 20 years and ideally before puberty. Children with xanthomata or other physical findings of FH or suspected to have HoFH by family history should be screened at 2 years of age. Children suspected to have HeFH should be screened between the ages of 5 and 10 years. It is recommended that two fasting LDL cholesterol values are obtained in children and adolescents. A high probability of FH is indicated by LDL of greater than or equal to 155 mg/dL or greater than or equal to 135 mg/dL with a parental history of hypercholesterolemia or premature CHD.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypercholesterolemia -- Evaluation -- Screening recommendations:
retrieved_docs.content: Men older than 35 Women older than 45 Presence of diabetes Tobacco use Family history of cardiac disease Personal history of heart disease or peripheral vascular disease Obesity (BMI > 30) Hypertension

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperlipidemia screening is done using blood tests in either fasting or non-fasting state. Universal pediatric screening at age 9 to 11 and repeated at age 17 to 21 is important for diagnosing familial hypercholesterolemia. For patients with a history of smoking, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and a family history of premature atherosclerotic disease, screening should begin at age 20 if they were not tested between ages 17 and 21 years as per the pediatric universal screening guidelines. For patients without the above risk factors, screening can begin at age 35 for men and 45 for women. For ASCVD risk stratification and intervention, LDL cholesterol values are most relevant. If screening results are normal, the testing can be repeated every five years. It can be repeated sooner (every year) in patients with abnormal values near the intervention threshold.

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia -- History and Physical -- Family History
retrieved_docs.content: Careful family history is essential for the assessment and diagnosis of familial hypercholesterolemia. Family history of premature coronary artery disease or high cholesterol in first-degree relatives (younger than 60 years in women and 55 years in men among first-degree relatives) are red flags. [22] It should also raise the suspicion for familial hypercholesterolemia with young patients showing early coronary events in second-degree relatives. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Dyslipidemia -- Introduction -- Screening for Dyslipidemia
retrieved_docs.content: During puberty, LDL cholesterol levels may drop physiologically. Hence screening is recommended in this age group. Universal screening is important because family history may not be reliable, and early diagnosis can make a substantial difference. For example, although the risk of premature cardiovascular disease is 20 times higher in familial hypercholesterolemia, only 20% of familial hypercholesterolemia is diagnosed.

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia -- Evaluation -- Screening for Familial Hypercholesterolemia
retrieved_docs.content: Screening for familial hypercholesterolemia should occur in people age 2 years and older if the patient has a family history of hypercholesterolemia or premature CHD. [14] Universal screening, including measurement of a non-fasting non-high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol or fasting lipid profile, is recommended to diagnose children with familial hypercholesterolemia at ages 9 to 11 years. [24] Children with a non-fasting non-HDL cholesterol result 145 mg/dL and higher need to be evaluated by fasting lipid profile. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia, Type 2A(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Testing should not be done during acute illness because it can lower LDL-C levels leading to false-negative testing. If already on statin therapy, it is important to look at pre-treatment values of LDL-C. If not available, the LDL-C level should be estimated based on the dose of the specific statin. Cardiovascular risk assessment tools such Framingham risk score should not be used as FH is a lifetime coronary risk equivalent. In asymptomatic patients with FH, cardiac CT and carotid ultrasound may be useful for stratifying risk and needs further studies. Criteria for the clinical diagnosis of homozygous FH include:

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia, Type 2A(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of FH in most cases is made phenotypically. Available clinical tools to make the diagnosis of FH are the Dutch Lipid Clinic Network criteria, Simon Broome criteria (Table 1), Make Early Diagnosis to Prevent Early Deaths (MED-PED) criteria, and Japanese FH criteria. [11] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Dyslipidemia -- Introduction -- Screening for Dyslipidemia
retrieved_docs.content: Selective screening: This is done for children with risk factors. Screening is recommended in younger children (over two years of age) in the presence of the following: family history of hypercholesteremia or premature coronary heart disease (myocardial infarction, coronary bypass surgery in men under 55 years and women under 65 years), or presence of risk factors such as obesity, diabetes, and hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Cataract -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Familial hypercholesterolemia

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia -- Evaluation -- Genetic Tests or Clinical Criteria/Diagnostic Criteria for Heterozygous FH (HeFH) and Homozygous FH(HoFH)
retrieved_docs.content: Heterozygous FH (HeFH): The two most commonly used criteria for evaluating and diagnosing FH are the Dutch lipid clinic criteria and the Simon Broome criteria. [26] [27] Both incorporate LDL levels, presence of xanthomas, the presence of a genetic mutation or family history of FH, premature cardiovascular events, tendinous xanthomas, or corneal arcus senilis, and elevated LDL levels in young ages. The Dutch lipid clinic criteria categorize patients into definite, probable, possible, or unlikely FH, while Simon Broome classifies patients as definite or possible. Having only an LDL level of 330 mg/dl or more, or a mutation in the LDL receptor, ApoB-100, or the PCSK9 gene will earn a diagnosis of probable FH, based on the Dutch lipid clinic criteria. If a patient has any of the additional factors of the Dutch lipid clinic, on top of the gene mutation or the LDL level, as mentioned above, the diagnosis becomes definite FH. Based on the Simon Broom criteria, a diagnosis of definite FH requires an LDL level of at least 155 mg/dl and a gene mutation or tendinous xanthomas in the patient or first- or second-degree relatives. Generally, genetic screening for FH is not required for diagnosis, but it is helpful when the diagnosis is uncertain. However, approximately 20% of patients with FH are diagnosed with clinical criteria, and genetic tests are negative for mutations. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia, Type 2A(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to exclude secondary causes of hypercholesterolemia such as hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome, and liver disease by history and laboratory testing. Diagnosis of FH in most cases is: Clinical , by a personal history of early CAD, family history of premature CAD (definite MI or sudden death, at an age younger than 55 in male first-degree relative or younger than 65 in a first-degree female relative) Physical exam findings (tendon xanthomas and premature corneal arcus) Elevated lipid levels (LDL-C greater than 190 mg/dL)

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia, Type 2A(Archived) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Cascade screening is a cost-effective, evidence-based intervention, starting with first-degree relatives of patients with FH by cholesterol screening with or without genetic testing. The use of genetic testing in cascade screening improves the detection rate of FH. Cascade screening can lead to early diagnosis and institution of therapy for FH resulting in ASCVD reduction. National and international organizations have recommended cascade screening for FH. Concerns regarding privacy require that the patient with FH make the first contact with family members. For patients diagnosed with FH, the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act protects against health insurance and employment discrimination.

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Combined Hyperlipidemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify patients at risk of familial combined hyperlipidemia through comprehensive medical history, physical examination, and familial screening. Assess lipid profiles, including total cholesterol, triglycerides, LDL cholesterol, and HDL cholesterol levels, at regular intervals to monitor disease progression and treatment effectiveness. Apply pharmacological and non-pharmacological interventions to control lipid levels and mitigate the risk of cardiovascular complications. Communicate effectively with patients, families, and other healthcare team members to ensure a clear understanding of the diagnosis, treatment goals, and follow-up plans. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Familial hypercholesterolemia is a common cause of premature cardiovascular events in children and adults. Various diagnostic criteria of familial hypercholesterolemia are available online and can help direct further workup. Heterozygous FH is underrecognized and commonly treated sub-optimally. Screening of first-degree relatives of patients with FH is essential in the primary prevention of CV events. Referral to a lipid specialist can help optimize the control of LDL hypercholesterolemia in patients with FH. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Lomitapide -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Dyslipidemia is a risk factor for atherosclerosis and could cause asymptomatic coronary artery or peripheral arterial diseases. Causes of dyslipidemia include sedentary living with diets rich in calories, saturated and trans fats, cholesterol, and familial (genetic) abnormalities in metabolizing fats. The evaluation begins by measuring serum lipid levels, including total cholesterol, triglycerides, HDL-C, quantified LDL-C, and VLDL. Screening exams should occur at ages 9 to 11, then at ages 17 to 21, and ages 2 to 8 (for a significant family history of severe hyperlipidemia, premature coronary artery disease, or other risk factors). Adults should have screening starting at age 20 and then every subsequent 5 years. Total and HDL cholesterol are assessable while not fasting; however, most patients should have all lipids assessed during fasting, often for 12 hours, for optimal validity and precision.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1214
retrieved_docs.content: Autosomal dominant inheritance is the mode of transmission for فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (hyperlipidemia type IIa). This genetic pattern means that a single copy of the altered gene inherited from either parent is sufficient to cause the disorder. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is characterized by markedly elevated low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, which increase the risk of premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Because the condition follows an autosomal dominant pattern, it often appears in successive generations, and first‑degree relatives of an affected individual have a 50 % chance of inheriting the same lipid abnormality. Recognizing the autosomal dominant inheritance of فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is essential for family screening, early diagnosis, and implementation of lipid‑lowering therapies to reduce cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1931
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is closely linked to several cardiac conditions and risk factors. It is clinically associated with cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي), hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم), and the presence of a heart murmur (نفخة قلبية). Hypercholesterolemia also has a clinical association with cardiac disorder, underscoring its role as a common metabolic risk factor for both entities. Diagnostic evaluation of coronary artery disease frequently employs coronary computed tomography angiography (تصوير الأوعية التاجية بالفحص المقطعي المحوسب), which itself is related to the broader concept of coronary disease (مرض التاجي). In the hierarchical structure of cardiovascular terminology, the parent concept “أم دم الشريان التاجي” encompasses cardiac disorder as a narrower element and includes coronary disease as a child, indicating that both the specific cardiac disorder and coronary disease are subcategories within this overarching category. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in recognizing the shared pathophysiology, risk profiling, and appropriate imaging strategies for patients with coronary artery disease and related cardiac abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 435
retrieved_docs.content: "Above reference range" serves as an interpretation used when evaluating "disorders of lipid metabolism." The category "disorders of lipid metabolism" encompasses several more specific entities. It includes the subcategory "Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën" (metabolic disorders of lipoproteins and other lipidemias), which in turn has a child concept "فرط ليبيدات الدم" (hyperlipidemia). Within the broader disorder group, the condition "فرط البروتينات الشحمية" (hyperlipoproteinemia) is recognized as a type of disorder of lipid metabolism, and the specific disorder "فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ" (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is directly mapped to the same overarching category. "فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ" follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, as indicated by the relationship with "Autosomal dominant inheritance." This hierarchical and inheritance information clarifies how various lipid metabolism disorders are related, how they are interpreted in clinical laboratory results, and the genetic considerations important for patient diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1212
retrieved_docs.content: Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën, a broad classification of metabolic disorders affecting lipoproteins and other lipid abnormalities, includes the specific condition فرط ليبيدات الدم, which denotes hyperlipidemia characterized by elevated blood lipid levels. Recognizing فرط ليبيدات الدم as a child entity within this larger category helps clinicians organize lipid disorders and guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 669
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are recognized as a specific form of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and are also classified under hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). Within this category, elevated blood glucose and new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)) are considered member conditions, highlighting the close link between lipid abnormalities and early disturbances in carbohydrate metabolism. Laboratory results that are “above reference range” are interpreted as indicative of a disorder of lipid metabolism, underscoring the diagnostic relevance of lipid panels. The disorder “فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ” (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is mapped to the broader concept of disorders of lipid metabolism and is known to follow an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, indicating that a single copy of the pathogenic allele can transmit the condition. Moreover, carbohydrate metabolism disorders (اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات) are positioned as a parent category to metabolic disorders (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي), reflecting the hierarchical organization of metabolic disease entities. Together, these relationships illustrate how lipid metabolism disorders intersect with broader metabolic pathways, genetic inheritance, and clinical interpretation, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1761
retrieved_docs.content: When laboratory results are reported as “Above reference range,” clinicians interpret this finding as indicative of a disorder of lipid metabolism. Hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) is a specific condition that falls under the broader category of disorders of lipid metabolism. Recognizing that an above‑reference‑range result may signal hyperlipoproteinemia helps guide further diagnostic evaluation and appropriate management of patients with abnormal lipid profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic conditions that include hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) as a specific type of lipid disorder. Within this category, elevated blood glucose and new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)) are also considered members, highlighting the clinical overlap between dyslipidemia and early glucose dysregulation. The disorder group is mapped to familial hyperlipidemia type IIa (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ), a well‑characterized lipid abnormality that follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, as indicated by the relationship between Autosomal dominant inheritance and فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ. This interconnected framework underscores how lipid metabolism disorders, specific hyperlipoproteinemia subtypes, and early diabetic manifestations are linked both pathophysiologically and genetically, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1608
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are classified as a form of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and include hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). This category is mapped to type IIa hyperlipidemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ), a condition that follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern (Autosomal dominant inheritance). Recognizing that type IIa hyperlipidemia falls under the broader umbrella of lipid metabolism disorders and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians with diagnosis, genetic counseling, and the management of patients who present with elevated blood lipids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 529
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic conditions that encompass several specific entities. Within this category, اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي (metabolic disorder) and فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia) are recognized as subtypes, indicating that these conditions are forms of lipid metabolism disorders. Additionally, ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) (elevated blood glucose/new‑onset diabetes) is listed as a member of disorders of lipid metabolism, highlighting the clinical overlap between dyslipidemia and early diabetes. The condition فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is mapped to disorders of lipid metabolism and is known to follow an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, emphasizing the genetic basis of this specific lipid disorder. Furthermore, اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات (carbohydrate metabolism disorder) is identified as having اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي as its parent, linking carbohydrate metabolic abnormalities to the broader metabolic disorder framework. Together, these relationships illustrate how lipid and carbohydrate metabolic disorders are interrelated, how specific lipid abnormalities can be genetically inherited, and how they may present clinically alongside early diabetic changes, informing a comprehensive approach to patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism encompass conditions such as فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia). Within this broader category, فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is a specific manifestation that is mapped to the class of lipid‑metabolism disorders. Type IIa hyperlipidemia follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that Autosomal dominant inheritance is the genetic transmission mechanism for this particular disorder. Recognizing that فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is a form of فرط البروتينات الشحمية and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians diagnose, screen families, and manage patients with lipid metabolism abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2832
retrieved_docs.content: The cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) is clinically associated with coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي), indicating that patients with coronary atherosclerosis frequently exhibit cardiac abnormalities and that the presence of a cardiac disorder may suggest underlying coronary artery disease, a relationship that is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2049
retrieved_docs.content: The term “vrozené” (congenital) is an allowed qualifier for both “فرط البروتينات الشحمية” and “انفصال الشبكية,” indicating that these conditions can be described as congenital forms. Recognizing the congenital nature of hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) and retinal detachment (انفصال الشبكية) is important for accurate diagnosis, genetic counseling, and tailored patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and chest pain (ألم الصدر) are both clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). This relationship indicates that elevated blood cholesterol levels can contribute to the development of acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and may manifest as chest discomfort. Recognizing hypercholesterolemia as an underlying factor for these cardiac presentations underscores the importance of lipid screening and management in patients who exhibit chest pain or anginal symptoms, thereby aiding in risk stratification and preventive cardiovascular care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2216
retrieved_docs.content: Medium chain triglycerides are contraindicated in patients who have ارتفاع نسبة الدهون الثلاثية في الدم (elevated blood triglyceride levels), indicating that the use of medium chain triglycerides should be avoided in individuals with this condition because it may exacerbate or is not recommended for managing high triglyceride concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2603
retrieved_docs.content: Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën (metabolic disorders of lipoproteins and other lipidemias) is classified as a subcategory of disorders of lipid metabolism. Within the broader group of disorders of lipid metabolism, فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia) is recognized as a specific type, and the category also includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) (elevated blood glucose / new‑onset diabetes) as one of its members. Disorders of lipid metabolism are mapped to فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia), a condition that follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. Additionally, Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën has a child concept, فرط ليبيدات الدم (hyperlipidemia), further linking it to the spectrum of lipid‑related disorders. Together, these relationships illustrate how various lipid metabolism disorders—including hyperlipoproteinemia, type IIa hyperlipidemia, and hyperlipidemia—are organized hierarchically, share common metabolic pathways, and may be associated with related conditions such as new‑onset diabetes, with specific genetic inheritance patterns informing patient risk assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1591
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) is clinically associated with both coronary artery sclerosis (تصلب الشريان التاجي) and hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). Coronary artery sclerosis (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is also clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) and is linked to coronary vessel imaging by computed tomography (تصوير الأوعية التاجية بالفحص المقطعي المحوسب). The CT coronary angiography (تصوير الأوعية التاجية بالفحص المقطعي المحوسب) in turn has a relationship with coronary disease (مرض التاجي). The entity “أم دم الشريان التاجي” has a narrower relationship with cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) and functions as a parent concept that includes coronary disease (مرض التاجي) as its child. Together, these connections underscore the role of elevated blood cholesterol in the development of both cardiac disorder and coronary artery sclerosis, the diagnostic importance of CT coronary imaging for identifying coronary disease, and the hierarchical link between the coronary artery blood source (“أم دم الشريان التاجي”) and coronary disease.
